novel_features.name: Local walk history
novel_features.description: Keep a permanent local record of every walk — distance, steps, time — that survives the belt's power-cut memory loss.
novel_features.rationale: The belt only stores its last run and loses it on power-cut; recording walks to a local store is only possible with a persistent local database the belt and phone app don't keep.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Reach for this when an agent needs durable walk totals the device itself cannot retain.

novel_features.name: Streak tracking
novel_features.description: See your current consecutive-day walking streak.
novel_features.rationale: Requires a cross-day join over local history that no single belt query can answer.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use when motivation/consistency tracking matters; the belt has no concept of streaks.

novel_features.name: Daily trends
novel_features.description: Distance, steps, and active minutes per day over a window.
novel_features.rationale: Requires historical daily rollups stored locally over time.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use to summarize walking activity over weeks for an agent or dashboard.

novel_features.name: Calorie estimate
novel_features.description: Estimate calories burned from your body profile — the number the belt refuses to report.
novel_features.rationale: The WalkingPad firmware does not report calories at all; they must be computed from a weight/age/height/sex profile and walk telemetry.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use when an agent needs an energy-expenditure figure the device cannot provide.
novel_features.group: Insight the device can't give you

novel_features.name: Family-generic discovery
novel_features.description: Find any compatible WalkingPad by its BLE service, not its name.
novel_features.rationale: Models advertise different names (a C2 shows as 'KS-BLC2', not 'WalkingPad'); matching the shared fe00 service UUID works across the whole family.
novel_features.why_it_matters: Use to locate the pad reliably across A1/C2/R1/R2/X21 where name-matching fails.

narrative.headline: Control any WalkingPad treadmill from your terminal — with a local walk history, calories, and agent-native output no WalkingPad app has.
narrative.value_prop: A single static binary (no Python, no cloud, no phone app) that drives the belt over Bluetooth: `run` holds the connection to walk the belt at any speed and records the session; `status`/`monitor` read live telemetry. It keeps a permanent local record of every walk the belt itself loses on power-cut, computes the calories the firmware never reports, and exposes every action as agent-native JSON. Physical-effect commands are safe-by-default: preview with --dry-run, confirm with --confirm-physical-effect.
narrative.auth_narrative: No API key or account. The CLI talks to the belt directly over Bluetooth LE. Only one client can hold the connection, so close the official WalkingPad phone app before using live commands. Live BLE actions require --live; without it, commands run against a safe replay transport.

quickstart.comment: Check BLE adapter, permissions, and that the pad is reachable before anything else.
Find your WalkingPad by its fe00 service (works even when the name isn't 'WalkingPad').
Read current speed, distance, steps, and mode from the belt.
Preview a guided 2.0 km/h walk; drop --dry-run and add --confirm-physical-effect to actually run + record it (the pad must be awake).
See today's recorded walking totals from the local history store.

troubleshoots.symptom: commands are accepted but the belt won't start
troubleshoots.fix: Wake the pad physically (display on), then use `run` (not one-shot `start`) — the belt only runs over a held, polled connection, which `run` maintains.
Power on the pad and fully close the official WalkingPad phone app — only one BLE client can connect at a time.
The BLE link is wedged from another client; quit the phone app, toggle the pad's power, and retry.

troubleshoots.symptom: commands print 'would write ...' but nothing happens
troubleshoots.fix: Add --live to actuate; physical-effect commands also need --confirm-physical-effect (or --dry-run to preview).
Grant your terminal app Bluetooth access in System Settings -> Privacy & Security -> Bluetooth, then retry.

narrative.when_to_use: Use this CLI when an agent or script needs to control or monitor a KingSmith WalkingPad treadmill over Bluetooth without the phone app: starting/stopping the belt, setting a precise speed, reading live telemetry, or querying a durable local walk history (totals, streaks, trends, calories) that the belt and app do not retain.

recipes.title: Pre-flight health check
recipes.explanation: Confirms the BLE adapter, OS permission, verify-state, and pad reachability in one call.

recipes.title: Start a 3.0 km/h walk safely
recipes.explanation: Sets an exact 3.0 km/h (finer than the native 0.5 step) after an explicit physical-effect confirmation.

recipes.title: Record a walk and review it
recipes.explanation: Captures the live telemetry stream into the local history store as a session you can query later.

recipes.title: Two-week trend for an agent
recipes.explanation: Returns the daily series as JSON; pipe to jq to narrow fields.

recipes.title: Export for Apple Health
recipes.explanation: Writes daily.json + yesterday.json for an iPhone Shortcut to log Walking workouts into Health.

trigger_phrases: how far did I walk today
